course_description: Various economic models analysed using a number of mathematical techniques including differential & integral calculus & matrix algebra. Economic models include e.g. goods market & cobweb models, Cobb-Douglas, diminishing marginal utility functions, financial applications.

requirements.prerequisites: [Maths B or equiv. or MATH1040] and ECON1010
requirements.assumed_background: It is very important for students to have taken first year micro and macro economic theory courses. In addition, students are expected to know algebra, calculus and coordinate geometry in high school Maths B or have taken the UQ course MATH1040. This course uses mathematics as the tool to analyse problems in economics. Thus it builds upon (and is not a revision of) what was covered in Maths B and the first year core economics courses.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Students are required to sign-on to a tutorial group via mySI-net. Students are then required to attend their signed-on tutorial group each week. For tutorials, procedures are strict. Spaces are limited and therefore it is your responsibility to ensure you are electronically signed onto a tutorial session in mySI-net to be guaranteed a seat.

After mySI-net no longer allows you to change tutorial groups yourself (Teaching Week 2), you may only change groups by submitting a request in writing to and receiving approval from the Course Administrator. Further details regarding sign-on requirements can be found on the course website on Blackboard.

Tutorials Commence in Teaching Week 2.

PASS (Peer Assisted Study Sessions) commence in Teaching Week 2.

Students should check their timetable on mySI-net prior to the commencement of their classes to ensure they have the most up to date venue information, as sometimes late venue changes can occur.

PLEASE NOTE: There will be no lectures, tutorials or PASS sessions during Teaching Week 4 due to the Exhibition Public Holiday. Additionally, there will be no tutorials or PASS sessions during Teaching Week 8 due to the mid-semester examination being held in the lecture that week. Please see the Learning Activities section of this course profile (Section 4.1) for the timetabling implications of public holidays .

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The aim of the course is to apply various mathematical techniques to analyse economic models. The mathematical techniques include simultaneous equations, matrix algebra, differential & integral calculus. Economic models include e.g. goods market & cobweb models, Cobb-Douglas, diminishing marginal utility functions.

learning_outcomes.description: apply algebraic and graphical techniques to economic models.
solve systems of equations; apply simultaneous equation techniques to economic models.

understand the notation and terminology of matrix algebra; add and subtract matrices; find the transpose of a matrix; scalar and matrix multiplication; represent a system of linear equations in matrix notation.
write identity matrices; identify whether a matrix is singular or non-singular; calculate a determinant.
calculate an inverse matrix using elementary row operations, adjoint method; apply matrix inverses and Cramer's Rule to solve systems of linear equations arising in economics.
manipulate and graph power functions, logarithmic and exponential functions
recognise notations for derivatives; evaluate first and higher order derivatives; apply the rules of differentiation to complex univariate functions; calculate marginal economic values, elasticities.
determine and classify stationary points of a function and and use them to sketch economic functions.
determine first and second-order partial derivatives for multivariate functions; determine and classify stationary points for unconstrained multivariate functions.

recognise the notation for indefinite and definite integration; apply the rules of integration to various functions; integrate more complex functions using integration by substitution and integration by parts; determine definite and improper integrals; evaluate total cost/revenue functions and welfare applications.
perform constrained optimisation analysis using Lagrange multipliers and bordered Hessian matrices.
apply and understand the graphical approach to linear programming problems.

learning_activities.topic: Wed 18:00 Lecture 1: Algebra review : real numbers; exponents; algebra; equations and inequalities. Learning Objectives: 1 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 1); Shan (ch 1); HPW (ch 1); S&H (ch 1, 2, 3.1-3.3)

Wed 18:00 Lecture 2: Equations and applications : logic; set theory; characteristics of linear and non-linear functions; curve sketching; microeconomic applications; mathematical proofs. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 6 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 1,2); Shan (ch 3); S&H (ch 3); Wis (ch 3)
Tue - Thu Tutorial 1: Algebra review
Wed 18:00 Lecture 3: Graphs and functions Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 6 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 2); Shan (ch 4,5); S&H (ch 4,5 )
Tue - Thu Tutorial 2: Equations and applications
Wed Exhibition Public Holiday : No lectures, tutorials, or PASS this week.

Wed 18:00 Lecture 4: Matrix Algebra 1 : terminology and notation; scalar and matrix operations; system of linear equations; Learning Objectives: 3 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 7); Shan (ch 4); HPW (ch 6); S&H (ch 15); Wis (ch 4,5)
Tue - Thu Tutorial 3: Graphs and functions

Wed 18:00 Lecture 5: Matrix Algebra 2 : determinants; cofactors; adjoint method and Cramer's Rule for solving systems of equations. Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 7); Shan (ch 4, 5); HPW (ch 6); S&H (ch 16); Wis (ch 4,5)
Tue - Thu Tutorial 4: Matrix Algebra 1
Wed 18:00 Revision lecture for mid-semester exam Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 6
Tue - Thu Revision tutorial for mid-semester exam
Wed 18:00 Mid-semester exam (during lecture) Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 6
Tue - Thu Mid-semester exam during lecture : No tutorials or PASS this week

Wed 18:00 Lecture 6: Calculus 1 : rules for differentiation; determination of critical points; partial differentiation for multivariate functions; differentials; applications. Learning Objectives: 7, 8, 12 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 4, 5); Shan (ch 8); S&H (ch 6, 7); Wis (ch 6, 7)
Tue - Thu Tutorial 5: Matrix Algebra 2
Wed Mid-semester break : No classes this week

Wed 18:00 Lecture 7: Calculus 2 : unconstrained optimisation; concavity and convexity of functions. Learning Objectives: 9, 10 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 5); Shan (ch 9); HPW (ch12-14); S&H (ch 8-9.4); Wis (ch 8,9,13)
Tue - Thu Tutorial 6: Caculus 1

Wed 18:00 Lecture 8: Calculus 3 : integration; integration by substitution and by parts; welfare applications. Learning Objectives: 10 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 6); Shan (Ch 9 ); HPW (Ch 15); S&H (Ch 9.5-9.7)
Tue - Thu Tutorial 7: Calculus 2

Wed 18:00 Lecture 9: Calculus 4 : constrained optimisation of multivariate functions with equality constraints; Lagrange method. Learning Objectives: 11 Readings/Ref: Jacq (ch 5); Shan (ch 10); HPW (ch 17); S&H (ch 14); Wis (ch 12)
Tue - Thu Tutorial 8: Calculus 3
Wed 18:00 Lecture 10: Revision for final exam
Tue - Thu Tutorial 9: Calculus 4

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

Feedback may be obtained from the marking of the homework assignments and from consultation with the lecturer and tutors.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

Definition of Plagiarism

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25123 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course introduces students to relevant mathematical methods for application in Economics. A good understanding of these methods is crucial for students to comprehend important concepts in the second and third year core economics courses and other selected areas in economics. Thus it is highly recommended that this course be taken before enrolling in the second year micro and macro courses. The course is also a requirement for students in the Bachelor of Economics program.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

An additional textbook is recommended for this course to ease students into the subject. It comes with a useul and interactive MathXL program with questions and self-assessment tests to help student learning.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .

C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.

D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.

E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Tutorials

Tutorial questions relating to each lecture will be provided on the ECON1050 Blackboard site. Tutorials will be held each week on the work lectured in the previous week. Students will be expected to attempt the tutorial questions prior to attending tutorials.

Consultation

Consultation is your chance to speak directly with the teaching staff, one on one. A consultation timetable listing the consultation times/locations for all teaching staff will be posted on the course website on Blackboard under "Course Documents". There is no need to make an appointment, simply attend any staff member's scheduled session. Note however that consultation sessions held immediately prior to assessment times are likely to be busy.

Peer Assisted Study Sessions (PASS)

PASS offers students a fun and interactive way to review the important material covered in each lecture, practise "mock" exam questions and deepen their understanding of key concepts. PASS sessions are run by past ECON1050 students who excelled in the course and they are an excellent opportunity to obtain the student's persepctive on the course - what to focus on in revision, useful study techniques and advice for the exam. Sessions are informal - all questions and queries are welcomed and PASS leaders are happy to tailor the session to focus on the interests of the group. It is a great way for you to participate in group study, as well as make some new friends at university.

PASS sign-on will be available under "Contact" in mySI-net. Note that spaces are limited and only those students who are officially signed on electronically will be guaranteed a seat. Sessions are capped to ensure an optimal study environment. Overcrowding reduces the benefits and will not be tolerated. The sessions run for 50 mins and commence in Teaching Week 2 . PASS does not replace tutorials, and only those students who first attend their lecture and tutorial will gain the most benefit from PASS. Each week PASS focuses on the content learned in the previous weeks tutorial, giving you the opportunity to master any areas you wish to improve on.

For more information on PASS including materials, head to the PASS link on the ECON1050 Course Website on Blackboard.

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Semester mark no more than 0-19.99%.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Semester mark between 20% and 44.99%.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 2 is: 20%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Semester mark between 45% and 49.99%.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 3 is: 45%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Semester mark between 50% and 64.99%.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 4 is: 50%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Semester mark between 65% and 74.99%.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 5 is: 65%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Semester mark between 75% and 84.99%.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 6 is: 75%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Semester mark at least 85%.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 7 is: 85%
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

SPECIAL EXAMINATIONS

Where a student does not attend an examination, they will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination. If, however, you are unable to attend a scheduled exam due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate, you may be eligible to sit a special examination at a later time.

The special exam application form can be downloaded from here:

http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor//forms/exams/SpecAppForm.pdf

Information on grounds for applying for special examinations can be viewed here:

http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/grounds-for-applying-for-special-examinations

General information about special examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/special-examinations Please read this information very carefully.

Application Procedure:

Note that the midsemester exam is a SCHOOL EXAM and the final exam is a CENTRAL EXAM .

Mid-Semester Examination - s ubmit your application to Ms Lauren Summerson with appropriate documentation attached (e.g. medical certificate) no later than five (5) calendar days after the date the original exam was held . Do NOT submit applications to the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre or the Student Centre. The special mid-semester examination will be held within 2 weeks of the original examination.

End of Semester Examination - submit your application with appropriate documentation to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building no later than five (5) calendar days after the date the original exam was held . The special final examination will be held during 14-16 December.

An application made on medical grounds must be accompanied by an original medical certificate signed by a medical practitioner or registered nurse no later than two (2) business days after the examination and stating that the student was unfit to sit the examination on the relevant day.

An application made by a student who is incapacitated during an examination must be accompanied by an original medical certificate signed by a medical practitioner or registered nurse within one (1) business day of the examination. The student must inform the examination supervisor of their intention to apply for a special examination and sign a Disregard Performance declaration prior to leaving the examination.

An application made on non-medical grounds (i.e. based on extenuating or unavoidable circumstances) must be supported by corroborating information .

Application Rulings:

Application rulings will be notified to you by email to your University student email address. Special examinations are not granted where the executive dean or head of school was not satisifed that reasonable measures (steps) were taken to avoid the circumstance that contributed to missing or failing to complete the examination. This includes holiday arrangements (including overseas travel), misreading an examination timetable, social and leisiure events (including sporting and cultural committments other than at state, national or international representative level).

There is no provision to defer special mid-semester examinations. Students who have been ill or otherwise impaired for more than 14 days in the semester are advised to consider withdrawing from the course, bearing in mind the dates of academic penalty and financial liability and the grounds for withdrawal. This also applies to students who are unable to attend the original mid-semester examination and corresponding special examination.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/supplementary-examinations-2901 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Special & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (14-16 December).
